title: Aryabhata Turns the Earth
subtitle: In Kusumapura, a Young Man Makes the Sky Move
era: Gupta science, the Aryabhatiya composed 499 CE
moral: Courage is trusting what reason and measurement reveal, even against old certainties.

sources.citation: Aryabhatiya (499 CE); W.E. Clark (tr.), The Aryabhatiya of Aryabhata (Chicago, 1930); K.V. Sarma ed. (INSA, 1976)
sources.note: The primary text, dated by Aryabhata himself.
Definitive modern study; clarifies the pi/irrationality question.

panels.shot: low-angle medium-wide of Aryabhata on a Kusumapura rooftop under a star-filled sky

dialogue.text: Kusumapura, 499 CE. Under the quiet stars of Gupta Magadha, a young Āryabhaṭa dares to say: the Earth turns.

panels.shot: wide establishing shot over the Ganga and Pataliputra’s cityscape

dialogue.text: This is Kusumapura, ancient Pataliputra, on the Ganga. Under the Guptas, poets, physicians, metalworkers and sky-watchers crowd its halls and terraces.

panels.shot: medium shot in an indoor gurukula-like hall, side light from a lattice window

dialogue.text: Āryabhaṭa is born in 476. By his early twenties, his hunger knows one field: gaṇita and jyotiḥśāstra, number and the science of the lights.

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder close shot on Aryabhata’s hands drawing a circle and chords on a palm leaf

dialogue.text: He counts, he measures, he compares sky and sand-table. Circles, chords, and ratios begin to obey his patient hands.

panels.shot: medium-wide on a flat rooftop at twilight, horizon-level shot

dialogue.text: Night after night he climbs the roofs of Kusumapura, marking the rise of stars, the path of the Moon, the changing length of days.

panels.shot: extreme close-up on Aryabhata’s focused eyes reflecting moving stars

dialogue.text: The sky seems to whirl from east to west. But his mind asks: Is it the dome that spins, or the ground beneath his bare feet?

panels.shot: dynamic medium shot on a riverbank with a moored boat, low sunset light

dialogue.text: One day he watches a boat slip down the Ganga. From the deck, trees on the bank seem to slide past. The eye lies; motion can belong to the watcher.

panels.shot: slow push-in medium close as Aryabhata looks from boat to sky, thoughtful

dialogue.text: So he reasons: the Earth may be the boat, the stars the bank. The spinning sky is only the illusion seen from a turning sphere.

panels.shot: interior medium shot in a simple chamber with a low desk, lamp-lit

dialogue.text: At twenty-three, in the year 499, he shapes his work: the Āryabhaṭīya. One hundred and twenty-one verses, dense as iron, light as starlight.

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder medium close on the palm leaf as coded letter-numerals are written

dialogue.text: He gives numbers not as bare digits, but as syllables. Consonants mark tens and hundreds, vowels mark place. Poetry itself carries calculation.

panels.shot: medium shot of Aryabhata standing before a small circle of students in a courtyard

dialogue.text: He arranges his verses in four quarters: the gaṇita of arithmetic and algebra, the kālākriyā of time, the gola of the sphere, the gaṇitapāda of geometry and chords.

panels.shot: medium shot of Aryabhata pointing to a drawn circle with marked radii on the ground

dialogue.text: Piece by piece he builds a jya table, values of the half-chord, what later ages will call the sine. Twenty-four entries, ready for any triangle or arc.

panels.shot: close shot on Aryabhata writing a verse giving the circumference of a circle

dialogue.text: For the circle’s measure, he gives a rule. In his coded words: take 62,832 as the circumference for a diameter of 20,000. Pi, as we name it, near 3.1416.

panels.shot: medium close on Aryabhata pausing, stylus raised, reflective

dialogue.text: He does not boast of perfection. He calls this value asanna, approximate. Enough for observatories and builders, honest about the limits of rule and fraction.

panels.shot: medium wide of Aryabhata and a brahmin elder on a veranda, city and river beyond

dialogue.text: Around him, stories still speak of Rahu and Ketu, shadow demons swallowing the Sun. These tales comfort, bind ritual and sky.

panels.shot: low-angle medium shot as Aryabhata addresses a small learned assembly in a hall

dialogue.speaker: aryabhata
dialogue.text: The Earth’s shadow darkens the Moon; the Moon’s shadow darkens the Sun.

panels.shot: medium-wide of Aryabhata at a sand board tracing Sun, Earth, and Moon orbits

dialogue.text: He explains eclipse as geometry. The Moon enters Earth’s cone of shadow; the Moon’s own shadow sweeps across Earth. No dragon’s head, but aligned orbits and cones.

panels.shot: medium shot in the same hall, some scholars murmuring while Aryabhata remains composed

dialogue.text: Such words unsettle. Tradition holds, temples teach. Yet he does not curse the old tales; he simply sets shadows and spheres in their measured places.

panels.shot: medium close on Aryabhata reciting, background hinting at a rotating Earth diagram

dialogue.text: In the gola section he says it plain: the Earth revolves on its axis, turning once each sidereal day, while the stars stand almost fixed.

panels.shot: wide shot of a quiet monastery library as dusk falls, shelves of palm manuscripts

dialogue.text: His little book travels. Scribes copy its verses for monasteries and courts. Some curse its brevity, some praise its daring; many puzzle for years over its verses.

panels.shot: time-lapse style wide panel showing centuries passing as different scholars study the same text

dialogue.text: By the seventh century, Bhāskara I writes his bhāṣya, the first great commentary. Later astronomers in India and beyond carry Āryabhaṭa’s methods into their own tables.

panels.shot: soft-focus medium shot of Aryabhata older, seated calmly with a student at his side

dialogue.speaker: aryabhata
dialogue.text: Trust what you can weigh, count, and see, even when stories differ.

panels.shot: epic wide shot from high above Earth, hinting at a classical Indian map beneath

dialogue.text: Āryabhaṭa does not end the old world; he quietly turns it. His courage lies in letting the turning Earth and measured shadow speak louder than myth, and trusting reason to follow.
